original_abstract: While equity, diversity, inclusion, and Indigenization (EDI&I) have become central to the mission statements of large institutions like universities, research continues to show only marginal progress toward achieving these goals. This stagnation is often rooted in the structural nature of systemic racism and sexism, which remain deeply embedded in both the formal policies and informal norms of academic environments. Drawing on a critical analysis of two EDI&I data-gathering initiatives and firsthand experiences of institutional resistance, this article conceptualizes the phenomenon of "institutional gaslighting." We argue that universities often operate in a state of paradox, publicly championing the discourse of EDI&I while simultaneously employing strategic maneuvers—such as the "slowdown," "pushback," "shutdown," and "blowback"—that prevent the dismantling of systemic inequalities. By theorizing these dynamics, we provide a framework for advocates to identify, name, and navigate forms of resistance that arise even in settings that claim to prioritize social justice. Ultimately, we propose a politics of refusal as a means to move beyond symbolic gestures and advance meaningful structural change within large-scale institutions.
humanized_abstract: Equity, diversity, inclusion, and Indigenization (EDI&I) are widely invoked in the mission statements of institutions such as universities. Research, however, indicates little or no progress in this regard. Using a critical lens on two initiatives whose aim was to gather data related to EDI&I at the university level and narratives of experiences where members pushed back against various structural and discursive obstacles, this article identifies “institutional gaslighting” and describes four particular methods that contemporary universities employ to hinder change, such that despite openly professing their support for EDI&I discourses, they can succeed in frustrating efforts to eliminate forms of systemic bias. By theorizing the “paradox of the contemporary university”, this article also allows activism to proceed with full knowledge of the potential “obstacles” it may run into at the institutional level. Indeed, by naming such obstacles, activism can potentially counter them and arrive at the only practical answer to institutional obstruction, which is a “politics of refusal”.

By theorizing the “paradox of the contemporary university”, this article also allows activism to proceed with full knowledge of the potential “obstacles” it may run into at the institutional level.
